Systematic literature review: Quantum machine learning and its applications
===========================================================================================

Abstract
--------

[Quantum physics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/quantum-physics "Learn more about Quantum physics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") has changed the way we understand our environment, and one of its branches, [quantum mechanics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/quantum-mechanics "Learn more about quantum mechanics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), has demonstrated accurate and consistent theoretical results. [Quantum computing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/quantum-computation "Learn more about Quantum computing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is the process of performing calculations using quantum mechanics. This [field studies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/field-study "Learn more about field studies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") the quantum behavior of certain subatomic particles (photons, electrons, etc.) for subsequent use in performing calculations, as well as for large-scale information processing. These advantages are achieved through the use of quantum features, such as [entanglement](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/entanglement "Learn more about entanglement from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or superposition. These capabilities can give [quantum computers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/quantum-computer "Learn more about quantum computers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") an advantage in terms of computational time and cost over classical computers. Nowadays, scientific challenges are impossible to perform by classical computation due to computational complexity (more bytes than atoms in the observable universe) or the time it would take (thousands of years), and [quantum computation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/quantum-computation "Learn more about quantum computation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is the only known answer. However, current [quantum devices](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/quantum-device "Learn more about quantum devices from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") do not have yet the necessary [qubits](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/quantum-dot "Learn more about qubits from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and are not fault-tolerant enough to achieve these goals. Nonetheless, there are other fields like machine learning, finance, or chemistry where quantum computation could be useful with current [quantum devices](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/quantum-device "Learn more about quantum devices from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This manuscript aims to present a review of the literature published between 2017 and 2023 to identify, analyze, and classify the different types of algorithms used in quantum machine learning and their applications. The methodology follows the guidelines related to Systematic Literature Review methods, such as the one proposed by Kitchenham and other authors in the software engineering field. Consequently, this study identified 94 articles that used quantum [machine learning techniques](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/machine-learning-technique "Learn more about machine learning techniques from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and algorithms and shows their implementation using computational [quantum circuits](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/quantum-circuit "Learn more about quantum circuits from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or _ansatzs_. The main types of found algorithms are quantum implementations of classical [machine learning algorithms](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/machine-learning-algorithm "Learn more about machine learning algorithms from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), such as [support vector machines](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/support-vector-machine "Learn more about support vector machines from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or the k-nearest neighbor model, and classical [deep learning algorithms](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/deep-learning "Learn more about deep learning algorithms from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), like quantum [neural networks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/neural-network "Learn more about neural networks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). One of the most relevant applications in the machine learning field is [image classification](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/image-classification "Learn more about image classification from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Many articles, especially within the classification, try to solve problems currently answered by classical machine learning but using quantum devices and algorithms. Even though results are promising, quantum machine learning is far from achieving its full potential. An improvement in quantum hardware is required for this potential to be achieved since the existing [quantum computers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/quantum-computer "Learn more about quantum computers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") lack enough quality, speed, and scale to allow quantum computing to achieve its full potential.

1. Introduction
---------------

Currently, there is an ongoing challenge in the scientific world to create [quantum computers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/quantum-computer "Learn more about quantum computers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") capable of substantial advantages for certain problems compared to conventional computers. The automated tools improvement and methods to assist in the simulation and design of the corresponding applications are required along with the device’s development. Otherwise, one could end up in a situation where one has powerful [quantum computers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/quantum-computer "Learn more about quantum computers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") but very few adequate means to use them [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b1). On the other hand, scientific progress in materials creation, hardware fabrication, and disciplines such as [error correction](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/error-correction "Learn more about error correction from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and compilation have enabled us to create large-scale and increasingly fault-tolerant [quantum computers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/quantum-computer "Learn more about quantum computers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b2). Some of the main objectives to be achieved are improving simulation times in chemical compounds, leading to substantial reductions in drug creation times. Also, the creation of complex cryptographic systems will allow the creation of secure computers, guaranteeing internet security for all users and the design of new artificial intelligence algorithms currently housed in most devices, from better prediction and recommendation systems to new industrial ones [support systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/support-system "Learn more about support systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

Since the 90s, we have observed the proposal and development of various highly relevant algorithms that demonstrate the computational advantage over classical computation. Some of them are Grover’s search algorithm [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b3) and Shor’s algorithm [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b4) for integer factorization in [polynomial time](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/polynomial-time "Learn more about polynomial time from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), both of which significantly outperform conventional machines. Recently, the application area of [quantum algorithms](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/quantum-algorithm "Learn more about quantum algorithms from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") has expanded dramatically and provided efficient methods in areas, such as chemistry [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b5), [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b6), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b7), communications [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b8), solving systems of [linear equations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/linear-equation "Learn more about linear equations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b9), [physics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/physics "Learn more about physics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") simulations [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b10), [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b11), cybersecurity [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b12), [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b13), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b14) and machine learning [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b15), [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b16). One of the most emerging areas has been the machine learning field. With existing [quantum devices](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/quantum-device "Learn more about quantum devices from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and algorithms, quantum algorithms have already improved some classical processes, and, in contrast, classical machine learning is used to enhance quantum procedures.

Within the application of classical [machine learning techniques](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/machine-learning-technique "Learn more about machine learning techniques from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for improvement of the quantum world, recent studies show the detection of quantum [entanglement](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/entanglement "Learn more about entanglement from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with unsupervised training in fully and partially entangled structures [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b17). Furthermore, [deep learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/deep-learning "Learn more about deep learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") techniques allow us to reduce the noise produced in [quantum systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/quantum-system "Learn more about quantum systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b18) or determine the [structural properties](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/structural-property "Learn more about structural properties from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [molecular dynamics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/molecular-dynamics "Learn more about molecular dynamics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the quantum scenario [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b19). Regarding the design and implementation of hybrid [deep learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/deep-learning "Learn more about deep learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") algorithms, the use of hybrid graphical convolutional networks (QGCNN) has been proposed, which, compared to quantum [convolutional neural networks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/convolutional-neural-network "Learn more about convolutional neural networks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), the classical multilayer [perceptron](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/self-organizing-systems "Learn more about perceptron from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (MLP) and classical convolutional networks, can achieve better performances, for example, in high-energy physics (HEP) [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b17). In the finance application field, hybrid [neural networks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/neural-network "Learn more about neural networks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have been used for predicting Gross Domestic Product growth [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b20). In the healthcare field, the use of [Boltzmann](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/boltzmann-equation "Learn more about Boltzmann from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") machines for classifying patients with lung cancer [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b21).

Bringing all these ideas together, the topic of this article is the link between the different types of quantum [machine learning algorithms](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/machine-learning-algorithm "Learn more about machine learning algorithms from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and their applications: What types of algorithms are used? Where are these algorithms implemented? We will address these questions based on the literature published in these research fields. To achieve these objectives, we conducted a systematic review of the literature. This paper is organized as follows: Section [2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#sec2) presents the systematic review protocol, including its different aspects and necessary steps. Section [3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#sec3) presents the systematic review results, Section [4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#sec4) discusses the topic, and Section [5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#sec5) presents the conclusions.

2. Materials and methods
------------------------

To identify, evaluate, and interpret the studies available in the literature and taking into account the research questions proposed by the authors, the methodology proposed by Kitchenham et al. [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b22) is used, thus organizing it into planning, conducting, and reporting the study. The structure of this section will be distributed according to [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b23), [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b24), [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b25). First, the databases will be selected, then the research questions will be defined as well as the inclusion and exclusion criteria and the definition of the search string, and finally, the review process will be carried out.

While working on this systematic literature review, we used Parsifal ([https://parsif.al/](https://parsif.al/)) to manage traceability and Mendeley ([https://www.mendeley.com/](https://www.mendeley.com/)) as the bibliographic manager. On the other hand, two bibliographic reviews have been taken as a reference to structure this study, one of them from a general perspective of [quantum computing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/quantum-computation "Learn more about quantum computing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b26) and another one from a more specific context of quantum machine learning [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b2). Furthermore, to facilitate the reproducibility of this study, we provide a public record of the review procedure and steps in [https://github.com/DaveHub5/SLR_Quantum_Machine_Learning](https://github.com/DaveHub5/SLR_Quantum_Machine_Learning). There, the readers can observe the reviewed articles’ status through the PRISMA diagram’s different phases [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b27).

We collected in total 5497 articles [[Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#fig1)], 1962 obtained from IEEE Digital Library, 1261 from Web of Science, 2224 from Science Direct, 45 from IBM Quantum Network Papers, and 5 articles from other sources published between 2017 and 2022. IBM Quantum Network Papers is an index maintained by IBM [https://airtable.com/shr5QnbLgraHRPx35/tblqDKDgMVdH6YGSE](https://airtable.com/shr5QnbLgraHRPx35/tblqDKDgMVdH6YGSE). The query strings used for the search process were “quantum machine learning”, “quantum deep learning”, or “quantum neural network*”. At the end of the study, we identified 94 articles that met the inclusion criteria, as we explain below. A summary of the identification and selection of the articles included in this systematic literature review can be seen in the PRISMA diagram [[Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#fig2)].

### 2.1. Review planning

This review attempts to show solutions and improvements for the development of [quantum circuit](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/quantum-circuit "Learn more about quantum circuit from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") simulation tools, quantum circuit design, and the creation and adaptation of different algorithms for [quantum computation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/quantum-computation "Learn more about quantum computation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The paper aims to serve as an introduction to this emerging field, along with a discussion of recent advances and presenting the problems that remain to be solved [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b26).

The field of [quantum computing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/quantum-computing "Learn more about quantum computing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a recent field compared with classical computing. For this reason, we will focus on the advances and applications of the last six years (2017–2023). We will compare the different techniques used as well as the challenges. The journals used for the articles retrieval have been IEEE Digital Library, Web of Science, and Science Direct. Also, an index of articles related to the cited topic has been included. These databases were selected according to the following requirements: the database is general-purpose journals with a large number of articles, the database admits full-length searches or searches only in specific fields of the works, is one of the most relevant in the research area of computer science, and the database is available for us, the licenses held by the University of Salamanca. IBM Quantum Network Papers is open access to everyone but is notably different, it is an article collection that includes particular articles of interest to the company or includes work that uses IBM Quantum hardware and tools. We include this not-indexed database with ensured contrasted quality content because essential articles are not included in the other databases.

### 2.2. Research questions

This article will focus on and try to answer the following questions.

*   •RQ1: What type of algorithm is used? 
*   •RQ2: Do they use hybrid or pure [quantum algorithms](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/quantum-algorithm "Learn more about quantum algorithms from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")? 
*   •RQ3: What are the application domains? 
*   •RQ4: What are the metrics used to measure the algorithm’s success rate or solution proposed? What are the metric values reported? 
*   •RQ5: Are the experiments carried out on simulators or real [quantum devices](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/quantum-device "Learn more about quantum devices from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")? 

After the research questions are defined, we used the PICOC method proposed by [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574013724000030#b28) to define our review scope:

*   •Population (P): The [target group](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/target-groups "Learn more about target group from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for the investigation. In this study: Quantum Machine Learning. 
*   •Intervention (I): specifies the investigation aspects or issues of interest for the researchers. In our case, those aspects or issues provide support or analyze QML processes. 
*   •Comparison (C): the aspect of the investigation to which the intervention is being compared. No comparison intervention has been planned in our study. 
*   •Outcomes (O): the effect of the intervention. We are looking for Quantum Machine Learning improvements and implementations. 
*   •Context(C): the setting or environment of the investigation. In our case, they are those environments related to QML (in the industry, universities, etc.).

Quantum machine learning - Wikipedia Machine learning with quantum computers # Quantum machine learning This article is about using quantum algorithms to solve machine learning tasks. The most common use of the term refers to quantum algorithms for machine learning tasks which analyze classical data, sometimes called quantum-enhanced machine learning. **Quantum machine learning (QML)** is the study of [quantum algorithms](/wiki/Quantum_algorithms "Quantum algorithms") which solve [machine learning](/wiki/Machine_learning "Machine learning") tasks.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:5-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The most common use of the term refers to quantum algorithms for machine learning tasks which analyze classical data, sometimes called quantum-enhanced machine learning.[[5]](#cite_note-Nathan_Wiebe_2014-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) QML algorithms use [qubits](/wiki/Qubit "Qubit") and quantum operations to try to improve the [space](/wiki/Space_complexity "Space complexity") and [time complexity](/wiki/Time_complexity "Time complexity") of classical machine learning algortihms.[[8]](#cite_note-:12-8) This includes hybrid methods that involve both classical and quantum processing, where computationally difficult subroutines are outsourced to a quantum device.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Farhi-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) These routines can be more complex in nature and executed faster on a quantum computer.[[3]](#cite_note-:5-3) Furthermore, quantum algorithms can be used to analyze [quantum states](/wiki/Quantum_state "Quantum state") instead of classical data.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The term "quantum machine learning" is sometimes use to refer classical machine learning methods applied to data generated from quantum experiments (i.e. [machine learning of quantum systems](/wiki/Machine_learning_in_physics "Machine learning in physics")), such as learning the [phase transitions](/wiki/Phase_transition "Phase transition") of a quantum system[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-:10-15) or creating new quantum experiments.[[16]](#cite_note-Krenn_090405-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Knott_073033-17)[[18]](#cite_note-:6-18)

QML also extends to a branch of research that explores methodological and structural similarities between certain physical systems and learning systems, in particular neural networks. For example, some mathematical and numerical techniques from quantum physics are applicable to classical deep learning and vice versa.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Furthermore, researchers investigate more abstract notions of learning theory with respect to quantum information, sometimes referred to as "quantum learning theory".[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Qml_approaches.tif/lossless-page1-250px-Qml_approaches.tif.png)](/wiki/File:Qml_approaches.tif)

Four different approaches to combine the disciplines of quantum computing and machine learning.[[24]](#cite_note-AimeurEtAl_2006-24)[[25]](#cite_note-DunjkoTaylorBriegel-25) The first letter refers to whether the system under study is classical or quantum, while the second letter defines whether a classical or quantum information processing device is used.



## Machine learning with quantum computers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quantum_machine_learning&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Machine learning with quantum computers")]

Quantum-enhanced machine learning refers to [quantum algorithms](/wiki/Quantum_algorithm "Quantum algorithm") that solve tasks in machine learning, thereby improving and often expediting classical machine learning techniques. Such algorithms typically require one to encode the given classical data set into a quantum computer to make it accessible for quantum information processing. Subsequently, quantum information processing routines are applied and the result of the quantum computation is read out by measuring the quantum system. For example, the outcome of the measurement of a qubit reveals the result of a binary classification task. While many proposals of QML algorithms are still purely theoretical and require a full-scale universal [quantum computer](/wiki/Quantum_computer "Quantum computer") to be tested, others have been implemented on small-scale or special purpose quantum devices.

### Quantum associative memories and quantum pattern recognition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quantum_machine_learning&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Quantum associative memories and quantum pattern recognition")]

Associative (or content-addressable) memories are able to recognize stored content on the basis of a similarity measure, while random access memories are accessed by the address of stored information and not its content. As such they must be able to retrieve both incomplete and corrupted patterns, the essential machine learning task of pattern recognition.

Typical classical associative memories store p patterns in the

(

2

{\displaystyle O(n^{2})}
![{\displaystyle O(n^{2})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6cd9594a16cb898b8f2a2dff9227a385ec183392) interactions (synapses) of a real,  symmetric energy matrix over a network of n artificial neurons. The encoding is such that the desired patterns are local minima of the energy functional and retrieval is done by minimizing the total energy, starting from an initial configuration.

Unfortunately, classical associative memories are severely limited by the phenomenon of [cross-talk](/wiki/Crosstalk "Crosstalk"). When too many patterns are stored, spurious memories appear which quickly proliferate, so that the energy landscape becomes disordered and no retrieval is anymore possible. The number of storable patterns is typically limited by a linear function of the number of neurons,

{\displaystyle p\leq O(n)}
![{\displaystyle p\leq O(n)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/877329da78c34dad3a9bdad7329791aa6933a531).

Quantum associative memories[[26]](#cite_note-:7-26)[[27]](#cite_note-:8-27)[[28]](#cite_note-:9-28) (in their simplest realization) store patterns in a [unitary matrix](/wiki/Unitary_matrix "Unitary matrix") U acting on the [Hilbert space](/wiki/Hilbert_space "Hilbert space") of n qubits. Retrieval is realized by the [unitary evolution](/wiki/Unitary_(physics) "Unitary (physics)") of a fixed initial state to a [quantum superposition](/wiki/Quantum_superposition "Quantum superposition") of the desired patterns with probability distribution peaked on the most similar pattern to an input. By its very quantum nature, the retrieval process is thus probabilistic. Because quantum associative memories are free from cross-talk, however, spurious memories are never generated. Correspondingly, they have a superior capacity than classical ones. The number of parameters in the unitary matrix U is 



O
(

n
)
{\displaystyle O(pn)}
![{\displaystyle O(pn)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2833b4c361db498c2e71d4e05c53df8a65062226). One can thus have efficient, spurious-memory-free quantum associative memories for any polynomial number of patterns.

### Linear algebra simulation with quantum amplitudes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quantum_machine_learning&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Linear algebra simulation with quantum amplitudes")]

A number of quantum algorithms for machine learning are based on the idea of amplitude encoding, that is, to associate the [amplitudes](/wiki/Probability_amplitude "Probability amplitude") of a quantum state with the inputs and outputs of computations.[[29]](#cite_note-Patrick_Rebentrost_2014-29)[[30]](#cite_note-Nathan_Wiebe_2012-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Maria_Schuld_2016-31) Since a state of 



n
{\displaystyle n}
![{\displaystyle n}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a601995d55609f2d9f5e233e36fbe9ea26011b3b) qubits is described by 




2

n
{\displaystyle 2^{n}}
![{\displaystyle 2^{n}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8226f30650ee4fe4e640c6d2798127e80e9c160d) complex amplitudes, this information encoding can allow for an exponentially compact representation. Intuitively, this corresponds to associating a discrete probability distribution over binary random variables with a classical vector. The goal of algorithms based on amplitude encoding is to formulate quantum algorithms whose [resources](/wiki/Computational_complexity "Computational complexity") grow polynomially in the number of qubits 



n
{\displaystyle n}
![{\displaystyle n}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a601995d55609f2d9f5e233e36fbe9ea26011b3b), which amounts to a logarithmic [time complexity](/wiki/Time_complexity "Time complexity") in the number of amplitudes and thereby the dimension of the input.

Many QML algorithms in this category are based on variations of the [quantum algorithm for linear systems of equations](/wiki/Quantum_algorithm_for_linear_systems_of_equations "Quantum algorithm for linear systems of equations")[[32]](#cite_note-32) (colloquially called HHL, after the paper's authors) which, under specific conditions, performs a matrix inversion using an amount of physical resources growing only logarithmically in the dimensions of the matrix. One of these conditions is that a [Hamiltonian](/wiki/Hamiltonian_(quantum_mechanics) "Hamiltonian (quantum mechanics)") which entry wise corresponds to the matrix can be simulated efficiently, which is known to be possible if the matrix is sparse[[33]](#cite_note-33) or low rank.[[34]](#cite_note-34) For reference, any known classical algorithm for [matrix inversion](/wiki/Matrix_inversion "Matrix inversion") requires a number of operations that grows [more than quadratically in the dimension of the matrix](/wiki/Computational_complexity_of_mathematical_operations#Matrix_algebra "Computational complexity of mathematical operations") (e.g. 



O



(

n

2.373
)
{\displaystyle O{\mathord {\left(n^{2.373}\right)}}}
![{\displaystyle O{\mathord {\left(n^{2.373}\right)}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1aae64872678cfd84c60f52b8c789d8d67141fb0)), but they are not restricted to sparse matrices.

Quantum matrix inversion can be applied to machine learning methods in which the training reduces to solving a [linear system of equations](/wiki/System_of_linear_equations "System of linear equations"), for example in least-squares linear regression,[[30]](#cite_note-Nathan_Wiebe_2012-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Maria_Schuld_2016-31) the least-squares version of [support vector machines](/wiki/Support_vector_machine "Support vector machine"),[[29]](#cite_note-Patrick_Rebentrost_2014-29) and Gaussian processes.[[35]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-35)

A crucial bottleneck of methods that simulate linear algebra computations with the amplitudes of quantum states is state preparation, which often requires one to initialise a quantum system in a state whose amplitudes reflect the features of the entire dataset. Although efficient methods for state preparation are known for specific cases,[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) this step easily hides the complexity of the task.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQAs)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quantum_machine_learning&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQAs)")]

In a variational quantum algorithm, a classical computer optimizes the parameters used to prepare a quantum state, while a quantum computer is used to do the actual state preparation and measurement. VQAs are considered promising candidates for [noisy intermediate-scale quantum computers](/wiki/Noisy_intermediate-scale_quantum_era "Noisy intermediate-scale quantum era"). Variational quantum circuits (or parameterized quantum circuits) are a popular class of VQAs where the parameters are those used in a fixed [quantum circuit](/wiki/Quantum_circuit "Quantum circuit").[[40]](#cite_note-:2-40)[[41]](#cite_note-:3-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) Researchers have studied VQCs to solve optimization problems and find the ground state

![Image 105](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/643d2eea03135260bdaca209/6585cbd47ce157b3cb7f24bf_ai-generated-face%20profile.webp)

Machine Learning has two roles within quantum computing. On the receiving side, quantum computers use classical machine learning to optimize hardware operations, control systems, and user interfaces. But, on the giving side, quantum computers perform machine learning tasks and solve traditional machine learning problems. Focusing on the latter, some of the most commonly cited quantum machine learning applications are:

*   Enhance algorithms designed to solve optimization problems, which in turn have a broad range of potential applications across many industries
*   Accelerate classification tasks for large datasets, particularly related to image and speech recognition, with quantum support vector machines (QSVM)
*   Enable exploration of high-dimensional datasets, such as customer segmentation and anomaly detection, using algorithms such as K-means clustering
*   Reduce dimensionality, using principal component analysis, to improve feature selection and thus improve the visualization and analysis of data
*   Generate more realistic data for Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) and Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) when the data either doesn’t exist or needs to be augmented
*   Discover complex patterns and correlations using Boltzmann machines for unsupervised learning, which has utility in recommendation systems and natural language processing
*   Improve neural network training by leveraging interference, potentially saving time by reducing the number of iterations required to find the optimal parameters
*   Solve near-term quantum chemistry problems, particularly applied to discovering new drugs and developing new materials, but also applied to a broad range of other problems
*   Analyze data for features and anomalies, the latter of which has important implications for network traffic analysis and fraud detection for financial services
*   Optimize policies for autonomous systems, including self-driving cars and unmanned aerial vehicles (UAV), using reinforcement learning

Although quantum computing is still in its infancy, cloud-based quantum machine learning solvers are already available. Some are proprietary and commercialized, but there are also open-source quantum machine learning services that are freely available.

**What is Quantum Machine Learning?**
-------------------------------------

Quantum Machine Learning (QML) is an interdisciplinary field that applies quantum computing to traditional machine learning tasks. But, classical machine learning already does that, so quantum machine learning algorithms must propose advantages over their classical counterparts:

*   The basic unit of information, the [qubit](https://www.quera.com/glossary/qubit), is not a 0 or a 1, but a complex number; depending on several factors, a complex number is minimally several bytes, each of which is eight 0s and 1s.
*   Entanglement exponentially increases the number of complex numbers required to describe a system, which means exponential compression of classical data
*   Quantum computation is not sequential like classical computation; it is inherently parallel, which allows multiple solutions to effectively be discovered simultaneously
*   Theoretical research supports the possibility of achieving significant computational speedups over known classical approaches
*   Data can be mapped not only from classical sources but also from quantum sources such as the proposed Quantum Random Access Memory (QRAM)
*   Interference can be leveraged to not only increase the probabilities of receiving correct solutions, but also to potentially provide speedups over classical algorithms

A paper by D.P. García, et al, titled “[Systematic Literature Review: Quantum Machine Learning and its applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.04093)” reviewed 52 articles, identifying and describing 18 hybrid classical quantum machine learning algorithms as a result. These algorithms include Boltzmann machines, parameterized circuits, autoencoders, reservoir computing, support vector machines (SVM), and an assortment of neural networks. This diversity of algorithms highlights the potential breadth of real-world problems that might be solvable with quantum machine learning.

{{Deloitte-Webinar}}

**Quantum Advantage in Machine Learning**
-----------------------------------------

The term “quantum advantage” typically refers to speed, but the term is not strictly defined. When a claim is made that a computation can be performed in 200 seconds that would take a supercomputer thousands of years, that’s a claim of quantum advantage. But, that suggests that there’s only one classification of quantum advantage when, in fact, there are multiple potential advantages:

*   Speed. The “holy grail” of quantum computing is to solve problems that consume considerable time and significant classical resources.
*   Precision. Many hard problems can only be approximated classically, whereas quantum algorithms can offer precise solutions.
*   Complexity. Quantum algorithms may solve problems in fewer timesteps than classical algorithms, indicating greater efficiency.
*   Compression. Large datasets that require prohibitive amounts of classical memory may be mapped to a relatively small number of qubits.
*   Dimensionality. As an extension of data compression, quantum computers are a natural fit for finding patterns and relationships in high-dimensional data.
*   Sampling. Quantum computers can naturally sample probability distributions for a wide range of applications, including generative algorithms.
*   Interference. Constructive and destructive interference can be leveraged to increase the probability of finding correct solutions and decrease the probability of incorrect solutions.

It’s important to note that [quantum algorithms](https://www.quera.com/glossary/quantum-algorithm) are not guaranteed to be advantageous in any way. Nor can they be expected to realize all of the advantages above. However, the potential to realize these advantages exists, and some quantum machine learning solutions might prove to be exceptionally advantageous.

**Quantum Machine Learning Applications**
-----------------------------------------

Quantum machine learning (QML) use cases overlap two other major classifications of quantum computing applications: quantum simulation and quantum optimization. Near-term solutions for both incorporate hybrid classical-quantum algorithms that utilize classical neural networks. And anywhere you find a classical neural network, is a potential application of quantum machine learning, as well:

*   Molecular simulation, the original application of quantum computing, with use cases in drug discovery, material development, and climate modeling
*   Optimization problems, with particular interest in financial applications, but with broad applicability across many industries, especially those with supply chains
*   Natural language processing, including the quick and accurate interpretation, translation, and generation of spoken languages
*   Imaging, including the classification and identification of objects in pictures and videos, and with applications ranging from health care to surveillance to autonomous vehicles
*   Machine Learning model training, optimizing how artificial neural networks train on classical data to solve the problems above

‍

Specific examples of some of these use cases, broken down by industry, include:

*   Energy sector, including energy grid optimization, energy demand prediction, energy storage, energy storage distribution, and renewable energy integration
*   Manufacturing, including production scheduling, shift scheduling, resource allocation, and defect detection, but also the optimization of individual processes
*   Retail sales, including demand forecasting, inventory management, delivery route optimization, recommendation systems, loss prevention, and store layout optimization
*   Government services, including traffic optimization, public transportation routing, policy modeling and simulation, and public health modeling
*   Finance and insurance, including financial portfolio optimization, financial risk management, fraud detection, and trading strategy optimization
*   Aerospace and space, including trajectory optimization and flight data analysis, both terrestrial and extraterrestrial, and communication optimization
*   Environment and weather, including weather forecasting, disaster prediction, environmental modeling, and natural disaster response management
*   Health care services, including medical image analysis, diagnosis assistance, treatment plan optimization, early detection screening, and clinical trial design
*   Customer service and social media, including personal recommendations, sentiment analysis, support ticket routing, omnichannel experience optimization, and chatbots

Although no references are provided, an Analytics Insight article titled “[What is Quantum Machine Learning? Applications of Quantum Machine Learning](https://www.analyticsinsight.net/what-is-quantum-machine-learning-applications-of-quantum-machine-learning/)” proposes some novel quantum machine learning solutions. Some overlap what is commonly referred to as “quantum simulation,” but they also list human anatomy, space exploration, and cybersecurity.

Most cloud based quantum machine learning options are impractically small due to low qubit counts, but Aquila allows exploration of [Quantum Machine Learning](https://www.quera.com/machine-learning) with 256 qubits. Neutral atoms are a natural fit for solving [Quantum Optimization](https://www.quera.com/optimization) problems, and the two fields are intertwined, so the research prospects are very promising.

**Adapting classical machine learning to the quantum domain**
-------------------------------------------------------------

Many classical machine learning algorithms do not naturally map to the quantum domain. This may be for a number of reasons:

*   The algorithm requires a large number of shots, which may be significantly slower on a quantum computer
*   The algorithm seeks to optimize the parameters using methods such as gradient descent, but quantum measurements are noisy
*   Quantum algorithms do not yet support millions of parameters such as classical ones.

Because of this, new QML algorithms are being [developed](https://www.quera.com/machine-learning) to take advantage of the unique properties of quantum while sidestepping quantum’s limitations.

# Quantum Machine Learning: What It Is, How It Works, and More

Quantum computing promises a quantum leap in processing power that could have big potential for machine learning. Explore this fascinatingâand potentially game-changingâtechnology inside.

![[Featured Image]: A quantum algorithm researcher working on a project. ](https://d3njjcbhbojbot.cloudfront.net/api/utilities/v1/imageproxy/https://images.ctfassets.net/wp1lcwdav1p1/2R4w1hrkE90U3sS5LkeKR3/658f76d64e319decb53b1d5c359e0e1d/GettyImages-1344688300.jpg?w=1500&h=680&q=60&fit=fill&f=faces&fm=jpg&fl=progressive&auto=format%2Ccompress&dpr=1&w=1000)

Quantum machine learning uses the power of quantum mechanics and quantum computing to speed up and enhance the [machine learning](https://www.coursera.org/articles/what-is-machine-learning) done on the âclassicalâ computers we use every day. Engineers design quantum computers using the often counterintuitive laws of quantum physics, and these machines can store and process exponentially more information than tablets, smartphones, and supercomputers that power much of the modern world.Â

But quantum computers have a long way to go before being used daily. With that said, investment in quantum computing is increasing because in September 2024, the US Department of Energy announced $65 million in funding for projects revolving around this technology [[1](https://www.energy.gov/science/articles/department-energy-announces-65-million-quantum-computing-research#:~:text=The%20U.S.%20Department%20of%20Energy%20(DOE)%20today%20announced%20%2465%C2%A0million%20in%20funding%20in%20quantum%20computing%20for%2010%20projects%2C%20comprising%20a%20total%20of%2038%20separate%20awards.)]. Additionally, if youâre wondering whether quantum machine learning is the future, several companies have already begun offering quantum devices accessible through [the cloud](https://www.coursera.org/articles/what-is-cloud-computing), creating the opportunity for hybrid work that pairs quantum computing with classical machine learning.Â

In this article, youâll learn more about quantum machine learning, quantum computers, and what they might be able to do in the future. At the end, youâll also learn about cloud-based quantum computing tools available today and some courses to help you start your own machine-learning journey.Â

### 

## What is quantum machine learning?Â

Quantum machine learning uses algorithms run on quantum devices, such as quantum computers, to supplement, expedite, or support the work performed by a classical machine learning program. Also called *quantum-enhanced machine learning*, quantum machine learning leverages the information processing power of quantum technologies to enhance and speed up the work performed by a [machine learning model](https://www.coursera.org/articles/machine-learning-models).

While limited storage and processing capacities constrain classical computers, quantum-enabled ones exponentially increase storage and processing power. This ability to store and process huge amounts of information means that quantum computers can analyze massive data sets that would take classical methods significantly longer to perform. As a result, quantum machine learning leverages this out-sized processing power to expedite and improve the development of machine learning models, neural networks, and other forms of [artificial intelligence (AI)](https://www.coursera.org/articles/what-is-artificial-intelligence).

### Quantum computing explained

**Quantum computers** use quantum mechanics to produce processing power that far outperforms even the most cutting-edge supercomputers used today. While classical computers operate on the classical laws of physics and store information using [binary bits](https://www.coursera.org/articles/binary-code) (1s or 0s), quantum computers leverage the often confounding laws of quantum physics to store information on sub-atomic particles called quantum bits, or **qubits**, that can hold more data than their classical counterparts and be used for more complex computations.Â

Thatâs not to say that quantum computers will replace your laptop or tablet any time soonâor even at all. In the future, classical computers and quantum-enabled ones are more likely to work side by side due to the fact that each is better suited to different types of tasks. Furthermore, quantum computers are costly to build and maintain and are susceptible to **decoherence**, or the decaying of a qubitâs quantum state from such common environmental factors as temperature fluctuations and physical vibrations.Â

Quantum computing, then, has the potential to vastly improve computational processing power and turbocharge technological innovation. However, thereâs still work to do to make it reliable, cost-effective, and broadly applicable to our everyday lives.

![](https://d3njjcbhbojbot.cloudfront.net/api/utilities/v1/imageproxy/https://coursera_assets.s3.amazonaws.com/articles/purple-hash-pattern.svg?auto=format%2Ccompress&dpr=2&blur=200&px=8&max-w=252)

## Quantum machine learning applicationsÂ

From crunching massive amounts of [big data](https://www.coursera.org/articles/what-is-big-data-a-laypersons-guide) to powering transformative technological advances, quantum computing, and machine learning stand to make waves in the future. While quantum machine learning is still in its infancy, researchers and professionals use it in numerous ways. Some of these applications include to:Â

Develop new [machine learning algorithms](https://www.coursera.org/articles/machine-learning-algorithms)

Speed up already existing machine learning algorithmsÂ

Employ quantum-enhanced reinforcement learning, in which a machine learning algorithm learns based on its interactions within a quantum environmentÂ

Create quantum neural networks, which can operate at fewer steps and with greater processing speed than traditional [neural networks](https://www.coursera.org/articles/how-do-neural-networks-work).

Despite these intriguing applications, quantum computing and machine learning are still growing and changing. As a result, many other applications used to solve real-world problems will likely develop in the near and distant future.

### 

## Continue exploring cloud-based quantum machine learning toolsÂ

enriched_points.expanded_content: Quantum Machine Learning (QML) combines quantum computing and machine learning to enhance data processing capabilities, enabling faster and more efficient algorithms for handling complex datasets.

enriched_points.point_title: Intersection of quantum computing and machine learning
enriched_points.expanded_content: QML represents a convergence of quantum mechanics and classical machine learning, utilizing quantum algorithms to outperform traditional models in speed and efficiency for data analysis.

QML enhances data-driven decisions by processing large volumes of information rapidly, improving accuracy in predictions and insights across various fields such as finance, healthcare, and technology.
